items.description: Add public method farewell(name: String) -> String to Greeter struct returning exactly "Goodbye, <name>!"

rules_checked.reasoning: The item declares kind 'type' for a change that adds a method to the existing Greeter struct. This mirrors the existing pattern in the same plan (i002, also kind 'type', also a method addition) and the file itself, where greet(name:) and greet() are simple member methods on the Greeter struct — the change is a member-level addition to a type definition, which the 'type' kind category covers; it is not mis-declared as e.g. a new module or a protocol.

Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift is the exact file where the Greeter struct and its existing greet(name:)/greet() methods live. Adding farewell(name:) to this same file/struct is the correct home — no new module or misplaced location is introduced.

Greeter is a stateless formatter producing strings; farewell(name:) does the same kind of pure string-formatting work as the existing greet methods. No view/view-model layering exists in this codebase and no domain logic or data access is being smuggled into an inappropriate layer.

This is a single additional method on an existing, already-stateless formatter struct that already hosts near-identical greet(name:)/greet() pairs. Extending the existing Greeter struct rather than creating a new type is the right granularity; a new type would be unwarranted for one more formatting method with identical responsibility.
No protocol or abstraction is introduced by this item; it only adds a concrete method to a concrete struct. No speculative abstraction exists to evaluate.

farewell(name: String) -> String follows Swift API Design Guidelines and mirrors the existing greet(name: String) -> String signature exactly in shape and argument labeling, so the name and its parameter clearly carry method semantics consistent with the rest of the type.
The change adds a method within the same file/module with no new imports or cross-module references; dependency direction is unaffected and nothing is smuggled in from outside the declared scope.

items.description: Add public method farewell() -> String to Greeter struct returning exactly "Goodbye!"

rules_checked.reasoning: Item adds a method (farewell()) to the existing Greeter struct; kind 'type' is used consistently with the sibling item F-FAREWELL-i001 which follows the same pattern of adding a method to Greeter.swift's type. It correctly reflects a modification to an existing type rather than mis-declaring it as, e.g., a new module or standalone function.

Target Sources/Greeter/Greeter.swift is the existing home of the Greeter struct, which already contains the sibling greet()/greet(name:) methods. Adding farewell() here keeps related formatting behavior colocated in its correct module.

Greeter.swift is a plain stateless formatter type with no view or data-access layering in this codebase; farewell() is a pure string-formatting method with no domain logic misplaced in a view or view-model, matching the altitude of the existing greet() methods.

This extends the existing Greeter struct with a second no-arg formatting method (mirroring greet()/greet(name:) pattern) rather than introducing a new type; granularity matches the existing sibling method greet() which has the identical no-arg/with-arg split, so no new type is warranted.
No protocol or abstraction is introduced by this item; it is a concrete method addition with no substitution or test-seam need indicated.
farewell() -> String follows Swift API Design Guidelines: a noun-based method name returning a value, directly mirroring the existing greet() -> String method's naming and signature style.
No imports are added or changed; the method operates entirely within the existing Greeter struct in its own module, respecting dependency direction.
